title: A Scalable FPGA Architecture for Real-Time Decoding of Quantum LDPC Codes Using GARI
authors: Daniel Báscones, Arshpreet Singh Maan, Valentin Savin, Francisco Garcia-Herrero
abstract: In this work, we introduce a new hardware architecture for decoding correlated errors in quantum LDPC codes. The decoder is based on message passing and exploits the structure of the detector error model obtained through the recently introduced Graph Augmentation and Rewiring for Inference (GARI) method. The proposed architecture enables flexible scaling and can, in principle, adapt to any quantum LDPC codes using the GARI framework. It leverages resource reuse while maintaining a modest degree of parallelism, thereby reducing power consumption and area requirements, while preserving low decoding latency.   As a case study, the architecture was implemented on a VCU19P FPGA as an ensemble of three decoder cores targeting the [[144,12,12]] bivariate bicycle code, achieving an average latency of 596 ns per decoding round. This implementation consumes six times fewer resources than the previous GARI-based proposal, being the first reported implementation of multiple decoder cores for correlated errors on a single FPGA device. This enables better energy-conscious scaling of the quantum error correction layer on the classical side, reducing overall power consumption while meeting real-time constraints without compromising decoding accuracy under correlated errors.
summary: This paper presents a new FPGA-based hardware architecture designed for the real-time decoding of quantum LDPC codes. By utilizing the GARI method to handle correlated errors, the design significantly reduces hardware resource consumption and enables the deployment of multiple decoder cores on a single FPGA, paving the way for scalable, energy-efficient quantum error correction.
main_problem: Achieving low-latency, scalable, and resource-efficient real-time decoding for Quantum LDPC codes, specifically addressing the hardware bottlenecks caused by correlated errors and increasing code distance.
main_result: The authors developed an FPGA architecture that achieves an average decoding latency of 596 ns for a [[144, 12, 12]] code, using six times fewer resources than previous GARI-based proposals and enabling multiple decoder cores on a single device.
method: A scalable FPGA architecture using controlled parallelism and systematic resource reuse, implementing a message-passing (Belief Propagation) decoder based on the GAI (Graph Augmentation and Rewiring for Inference) framework.
model_or_system: The implementation targets Quantum Low-Density Parity-Check (QLDPC) codes, specifically the [[144, 12, 12]] bivariate bicycle code, under a correlated error model (X, Y, and Z errors).
key_observables: Decoding latency, hardware resource utilization (LUTs, REGs, BRAMs), and decoding accuracy under correlated errors.
important_parameters: Code parameters [[144, 12, 12]], clock frequency of approximately 274-300 MHz, and 6-bit to 10-bit LLR quantization.
main_assumptions: The routing efficiency assumes a uniform distribution of tags in the crossbar interconnect; the impact of reduced bit-width quantization on error floors is noted as an unknown.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the GARI architecture overview; Figure 2 details the DX, DZ decoder unit; Figure 4 illustrates the crossbar interconnect structure; Figure 5 shows the full decoder mapping; Figures 7-9 illustrate timing diagrams and physical placement on the FPGA.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the decoding problem and GARI framework, describes the proposed hardware architecture (DX, DZ and U, V units), details the interconnect and routing strategy, presents implementation results on Xilinx FPGAs, and discusses scalability and future work.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Overview of the GARI architecture from this work.
Fig. 2. The architecture for the DX, DZ decoder. Memory elements shown in blue (light for RAM, dark for ROM). I/O in purple. Sub-modules in gray.
Fig. 3. Architecture for the U, V decoder. Memory elements shown in blue (light for RAM, dark for ROM), I/O in purple. Sub-modules in gray.
Fig. 4. The crossbar interconnect. Note that the inputs and outputs are buffered in FIFO queues to relieve pressure from the input and output modules. Internally, ping-pong buffers suffice.
Fig. 5. Architecture for the full decoder. Note the straightforward mapping between DXDZ and UV tiles.
